quizzes.lessonTitle: Coating behaviour in Australian humidity and documenting the job
quizzes.title: Timber Floor Assessment & Restoration — Knowledge Check

questions.questionText: Which factor most determines how much a particular timber floor board will expand and contract as its moisture content changes?
options: The colour of the coating applied to the floor
The timber species and its inherent hardness and moisture-movement characteristics
The brand of moisture meter used to test it
Whether the floor was installed before or after 2010

questions.questionText: Compared with solid strip timber flooring, engineered timber flooring generally:
options: Holds moisture longer and is more tolerant of prolonged wetting
Cannot be tested with a moisture meter
Dries faster near the surface but is less tolerant of prolonged wetting, with a real risk of delamination between the wear layer and substrate
Is always installed directly over a suspended timber subfloor

questions.questionText: A timber floor shows edges higher than the centre along each board, consistent with the underside being wetter than the top. This distortion is known as:
options: Crowning
Buckling
Cupping
Permanent set

questions.questionText: Crowning (the centre of each board higher than its edges) is most often seen when:
options: A floor that was cupped is dried too fast from the top, or sanded flat before the underside has caught up and dried
The floor was never wet in the first place
A moisture meter reading is taken incorrectly
Subfloor vents are fully open and unobstructed

questions.questionText: What distinguishes permanent set from ordinary moisture-driven cupping?
options: Permanent set only affects engineered floors, never solid timber
Permanent set always looks more cupped than ordinary cupping
The distortion in permanent set does not relax even once the board is confirmed at or near the dry standard on a moisture meter, because the wood fibres have been crushed or damaged
Permanent set can only be confirmed after the floor has been sanded

questions.questionText: The "dry standard" for a timber floor restoration job is best defined as:
options: A single fixed moisture-content percentage that applies to every timber species
The moisture content reading of the same or a comparable species in an unaffected part of the same floor or building, using the same meter and settings
The moisture reading taken on the day the drying equipment is removed, regardless of what it shows
Whatever moisture reading makes the floor feel dry underfoot

questions.questionText: In a suspended timber subfloor affected by water damage, poor subfloor ventilation most directly increases the risk of:
options: Faster, more even drying of the floor above
Sustained subfloor moisture, extending the drying timeline and creating conditions favourable to termite activity
Automatically improved dry standard readings
A reduction in the load on electrical circuits used for drying

questions.questionText: During assessment of a subfloor void, a technician notices mud shelter tubes on the piers and soft, hollow-sounding timber. The correct response is to:
options: Identify the termite species and apply a suitable treatment before continuing
Ignore it and proceed with drying, since termites are not a restoration concern
Stop, document what was observed, and refer the client to a licensed pest inspector or timber pest technician before continuing structural work in that area
Increase air mover speed in the subfloor void to discourage termite activity

questions.questionText: Visible mould is present in a subfloor void beneath a water-damaged timber floor. The correct approach to airflow is to:
options: Run air movers in the void to dry the mould out faster
Do not run air movers across the affected area; contain, filter with AFDs and dehumidify first, and introduce air movers only after the area is cleaned and cleared
Treat the subfloor void differently from the room above, since the mould rule only applies indoors
Increase airflow to vent the spores out through the subfloor vents

questions.questionText: Why is timber floor drying generally staged, favouring dehumidification early and only introducing broader airflow once surface moisture has come down?
options: Because airflow is more expensive to run than dehumidification
Because drying the surface much faster than moisture can migrate up from the core creates a steep internal moisture gradient, causing splitting, checking or crowning
Because Australian standards prohibit air movers on timber floors under any circumstances
Because moisture meters cannot function while air movers are running
